text: The surgeon develops the retroactive space using a robotic eight millimeter trocar through the right-sided ports, navigating the area to access the hernia defects.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a Storz FiveZero camera to create space for the placement of a second trocar lateral to the semilunar line. The semilunar lines are marked bilaterally with ultrasound, and the bed is extended to separate the costal margin from the ASIS. A spinal needle is used to find the trajectory for the second trocar, which is inserted medial to the semilunar line. The robotic camera is then used for further dissection to accommodate a third trocar. The arcuate line and epigastric vessels are visible, and a bariatric length trocar is placed near the ASIS for better clearance.

The surgeon docks the robot and clears the midline from top to bottom, focusing on the lower midline crossover below the arcuate line. The rectus muscles are visible, appearing scalloped and forming a teepee shape. The surgeon exposes the contralateral rectus, then dissects through the posterior sheath above and below the arcuate line, working in the preperitoneal space below the umbilicus.

The surgeon opens the ipsilateral posterior rectus sheath and navigates from inferior to superior, reaching the subxiphoid area above the hernia defect. The crossing over through the hernia defect is performed carefully to avoid creating holes in the posterior elements, while maintaining close proximity to the fascial defects.

The surgeon enters the left-sided retro-rectus space, navigating across multiple Swiss cheese defects in the diastatic rectus linea alba. A small hole is made in the lateral peritoneum, causing gas loss into the peritoneal cavity. The surgeon applies pressure with the left hand to maintain the working space. Cold scissors dissection is used to preserve the peritoneum and hernia sac, avoiding thermal spread to the contents within the hernia sac.

The surgeon inspects the peritoneal hole to ensure no bowel is present, observing only omentum. The hernia defect is nearly reduced, with the hernia sac being carefully preserved to avoid large holes in the posterior elements. The surgeon demonstrates the 'volcano sign' to a student, indicating the complete crossing of the midline and clearance of all hernia defects. The left posterior rectus sheath is opened, allowing for the development of the left-sided posterior rectus and retrorectus spaces.

The surgeon carefully clears the surgical space, avoiding injury to the lateral neurovascular bundles and epigastric vessels. Dissection continues in the space of Retzius, focusing on upper dissection to accommodate mesh placement. The posterior component layers are examined for holes, and a two-ovelock suture is used to close them, preserving the posterior peritoneum and hernia sac without tension.

The surgeon begins anterior closure using a zero v-lock suture, starting from the top and moving towards the widest hernia defect. The pressure is reduced to facilitate tension-free closure of the anterior sheath. An image inversion technique is employed, switching camera orientation and instrument assignments, which aids in suturing. The scalloped rectus muscles are visible, complicating the view of the fascial opening. The surgeon uses the left hand to hold down the muscle for better visibility, completing the stitch near the pubic symphysis and tying it to another stitch.

The surgeon completes the closure of the hernia defect using barbed sutures and measures the space, which is approximately 32-33 cm long and 20 cm wide. A piece of heavyweight polypropylene mesh is cut to 30 by 20 cm and inserted through a 12 mm laparoscopic trocar in the right upper quadrant port. The camera is switched to the lower port to provide a view towards the right upper quadrant, where the mesh is flattened into place. The procedure concludes with desufflation, and no drain is left in place.
